masthead.title_html: Reason Your Way Through Europe, Vol. 2
masthead.title_plain: Reason Your Way Through Europe, Vol. 2
masthead.dek: A fresh round on the continent — physical geography, the political map's oddities, history, and culture. Every answer routes to a country, a decade, or a count, and every one defeats a reflex (the driest place in Europe isn't where you'd think). Narrow it down, place your bet, then tap to check.
masthead.footer_html: Built fresh against the existing Europe set — no repeated questions. Verified to Britannica, Eurostat, WorldAtlas, the ECB, and UNESCO.

plan.note: Volume 2: fresh axes (enclaves, double-landlocked, oldest parliament, dissolution dates, per-capita exports) chosen to avoid the first set's dimensions. Answers route to country, decade/century, or count, each with a reflex-defeater.

questions.cat: Europe · double-landlocked
questions.dimension: double-landlocked
questions.q: To reach the sea from this European country you must cross at least two borders, because every one of its neighbors is also landlocked. Which country is it?
questions.detail: The only country in Europe whose every neighbor is itself landlocked.
questions.why: To reach the sea you must cross at least two borders. In all the world only two countries are 'doubly landlocked'; the European one is wedged between Austria and Switzerland, both of which are also landlocked.
questions.more: The other doubly landlocked country is Uzbekistan. Liechtenstein has no airport and uses the Swiss franc.
questions.source: WorldAtlas, 'The World's Two Double Landlocked Countries'; Worldpopulationreview Double-Landlocked Countries 2026

questions.cat: Europe · capital bordering two foreign countries
questions.dimension: capital bordering two foreign countries
questions.q: Which national capital sits so close to its country's edge that its city limits run right up against the borders of two other countries at once?
questions.detail: The only national capital that directly touches two other countries' borders.
questions.why: Most capitals sit safely inland. This one hugs its country's edge so tightly that its city limits run right up against both Austria and Hungary.
questions.more: It sits on the Danube about 55 km from Vienna, the closest capital-to-capital pairing in Europe.
questions.source: Authentic Slovakia, 'Bratislava and Vienna: the Twin Cities'; Wikipedia 'Geography of Bratislava'

questions.cat: Europe · two simultaneous heads of state
questions.dimension: two simultaneous heads of state
questions.q: Which small European country has two heads of state at once, neither of whom lives there, with one being a foreign president and the other a foreign bishop?
questions.detail: A small country jointly ruled by a foreign president and a foreign bishop.
questions.why: It has not one head of state but two 'co-princes', and neither lives there: one is the President of France, the other a bishop from Spain. The arrangement dates to a 1278 treaty.
questions.more: It sits high in the Pyrenees between France and Spain and is landlocked.
questions.source: Wikipedia 'Co-princes of Andorra'; Royal Central, 'Why is the President of France Co-Prince of Andorra?'

questions.cat: Europe · country fully surrounding another country
questions.dimension: country fully surrounding another country
questions.q: Which European country completely wraps around two separate independent states, accounting for two of the only three such enclave nations in the world?
questions.detail: The country that completely wraps around two separate independent states.
questions.why: Worldwide only three nations are totally surrounded by a single neighbor, and one country accounts for two of those three: it encloses both San Marino and Vatican City.
questions.more: The third such enclave nation is Lesotho, inside South Africa.
questions.source: Interesting Facts, 'There are only three countries entirely surrounded by one other country'; WorldAtlas 'Countries That Are Enclaves'

questions.cat: Europe · second-smallest sovereign state
questions.dimension: second-smallest sovereign state
questions.q: Only one country on Earth is smaller than this Mediterranean state, which touches just France by land. Which country is it?
questions.detail: The world's second-tiniest country, bordered on land by just one nation.
questions.why: It is smaller than many city parks and touches only France by land, with the Mediterranean on its remaining side. Only one country on Earth is smaller.
questions.more: Its area is about 2 square kilometers; the smaller one is Vatican City.
questions.source: Britannica 'Monaco'; WorldAtlas 'Which Country Borders Monaco?'

questions.cat: Europe · last divided capital
questions.dimension: last divided capital
questions.q: Which capital city has been split into a southern and a northern half by a patrolled UN buffer zone since the 1970s, making it the world's last divided capital?
questions.detail: The only capital still split in two by a patrolled buffer zone.
questions.why: A UN-controlled line has cut this capital into a southern and a northern half since the 1970s, making it the world's last divided capital city.
questions.more: The buffer 'Green Line' runs about 180 km across the whole island.
questions.source: Vice, 'Life in the Last Capital City Still Split by a Border Zone'; Lonely Planet, 'Tracing history across Nicosia's Green Line'

questions.cat: Europe · country owning an exclave inside another
questions.dimension: country owning an exclave inside another
questions.q: High in the Pyrenees sits the town of Llivia, ringed entirely by French land yet belonging to a different country. Which country owns it?
questions.detail: The country that owns a small town completely cut off inside France.
questions.why: High in the Pyrenees sits the town of Llívia, which belongs to this country yet is ringed entirely by French land, separated from the homeland by about 1.6 km of France.
questions.more: It stayed with this country because a 1660 treaty ceded only 'villages' to France, and Llívia ranked as a town.
questions.source: Amusing Planet, 'Llívia: A Curious Spanish Enclave in France'; CNN Travel, 'Llivia: The Spanish town stranded in France'

questions.cat: Europe · country owning an exclave inside Switzerland (euro-
questions.dimension: country owning an exclave inside Switzerland (euro-free)
questions.q: The town of Busingen am Hochrhein is completely surrounded by Switzerland, sits outside the EU, and spends Swiss francs, yet its government is in another country. Which country does it belong to?
questions.detail: The country whose only town that uses the Swiss franc and skips the EU sits ringed by Switzerland.
questions.why: Büsingen am Hochrhein belongs to this country but is completely surrounded by Switzerland, so it sits outside the EU customs area and spends Swiss francs even though its government is back home.
questions.more: It is separated from the homeland by only a 750-meter strip of Swiss land.
questions.source: SWI swissinfo.ch, 'The curious case of the German village surrounded by Switzerland'; deutschland.de, 'German exclave in Switzerland'

questions.cat: Europe · country owning a tangle of enclaves inside the Neth
questions.dimension: country owning a tangle of enclaves inside the Netherlands
questions.q: In the twin town of Baarle, more than twenty little patches of one country's land are scattered inside the Netherlands, some with Dutch pockets nested inside them. Which country owns those patches?
questions.detail: The country that holds 22 little patches of land scattered inside a Dutch town.
questions.why: In the twin town of Baarle, this country's territory is broken into more than twenty enclaves dropped inside the Netherlands, some with Dutch counter-enclaves nested inside them.
questions.more: The border runs through houses and shops; some buildings have two national addresses.
questions.source: IFLScience, 'Baarle: A Town Between the Netherlands And Belgium'; France24, 'Welcome to Baarle, world's strangest border'

questions.cat: Europe · capital at a two-river confluence
questions.dimension: capital at a two-river confluence
questions.q: Which European capital is built at the point where the Sava river flows into the Danube, making it the only major capital at such a river junction?
questions.detail: The only European capital sitting where two great rivers meet.
questions.why: This capital stands on the point where the Sava flows into the Danube, the only European capital built at the junction of two such major rivers.
questions.more: Its old fortress, Kalemegdan, overlooks the meeting point.
questions.source: Britannica 'Belgrade'; Serbia.com, 'The Sava river'

questions.cat: Europe · European country with the most land neighbors
questions.dimension: European country with the most land neighbors
questions.q: Which European country shares a land border with more other nations than any other on the continent?
questions.detail: The European country that touches more nations by land than any other.
questions.why: Counting all its frontiers, this country borders fourteen other states, far more than any other in Europe and tied for the world lead.
questions.more: The next-highest in Europe is Germany with nine.
questions.source: WorldAtlas, 'Countries Bordering The Highest Number Of Other Countries'

questions.cat: Europe · Russian exclave wedged between two EU states
questions.dimension: Russian exclave wedged between two EU states
questions.q: Russia's Kaliningrad region is cut off from the rest of Russia, squeezed between Poland and one EU country. Which EU country also borders Belarus across the 'Suwalki Gap' there?
questions.detail: The single EU country that borders both Belarus and Russia's cut-off Baltic exclave.
questions.why: Russia's Kaliningrad region is detached from the rest of Russia and squeezed between Poland and this country, which is also the EU member sharing the strategic 'Suwałki Gap' next to Belarus.
questions.more: Kaliginrad was the German city of Königsberg before 1945.
questions.source: Britannica 'Kaliningrad'; Wikipedia 'Lithuania–Russia border'

questions.cat: Europe · count of Europe's landlocked countries
questions.dimension: count of Europe's landlocked countries
questions.q: How many European countries have no sea coast at all when you count microstates and the newer Balkan states?
questions.detail: How many European countries have no sea coast at all.
questions.why: Tallying every nation on the continent with no coastline gives a number most people guess far too low; the commonly cited modern figure includes microstates and the newer Balkan states.
questions.more: The list runs from Andorra and Austria through Serbia, Slovakia, and Vatican City.
questions.source: Worldpopulationreview, 'Landlocked European Countries 2026'; WorldAtlas 'Landlocked Countries In Europe'

questions.cat: Europe · country with one of Europe's oldest fixed borders
questions.dimension: country with one of Europe's oldest fixed borders
questions.q: Which country touches only one neighbor by land along a boundary fixed by the 1297 Treaty of Alcanices, one of the world's oldest essentially unchanged borders?
questions.detail: The country whose land boundary has barely moved since 1297.
questions.why: This country touches only one neighbor by land, and that line was fixed by the 1297 Treaty of Alcañices, making it one of the oldest essentially unchanged borders in the world.
questions.more: It is also the EU's longest continuous internal land border.
questions.source: Wikipedia 'Portugal–Spain border'; espaces-transfrontaliers.org border factsheet

questions.cat: Europe · Italian exclave inside Switzerland (route to capita
questions.dimension: Italian exclave inside Switzerland (route to capital)
questions.q: The lakeside town of Campione d'Italia is completely surrounded by Switzerland, yet its laws come from a faraway capital about 600 km away across the Alps. Which capital governs it?
questions.detail: The far-off capital that governs the lakeside town of Campione, ringed by Switzerland.
questions.why: Campione d'Italia sits on Lake Lugano completely surrounded by Switzerland, yet its laws come from the capital roughly 600 km away on the other side of the Alps.
questions.more: Reaching the rest of its country by road means driving about 14 km through Swiss territory.
questions.source: Wikipedia 'Campione d'Italia'; Slow Italy, 'Campione d'Italia, Italy's only exclave'

questions.cat: Europe · world's oldest surviving republic
questions.dimension: world's oldest surviving republic
questions.q: Which mountaintop microstate, surrounded entirely by one larger neighbor, claims by tradition to be the oldest surviving republic on Earth?
questions.detail: A mountaintop microstate that claims to be the oldest republic on Earth.
questions.why: Founded by tradition in 301 AD and surrounded entirely by a single larger neighbor, it claims the title of the world's oldest still-standing republic.
questions.more: It sits atop Monte Titano and is enclosed by Italy.
questions.source: New World Encyclopedia 'San Marino'; NASA Earth Observatory, 'San Marino Enclave in Italy'

questions.cat: Europe · smallest sovereign state by area
questions.dimension: smallest sovereign state by area
questions.q: Which is the smallest independent country in the world by area, tucked entirely within the city of Rome and largely ringed by walls?
questions.detail: The world's tiniest country, walled inside one city.
questions.why: At under half a square kilometer it is the smallest independent state on the planet, tucked entirely within the city of Rome and largely ringed by walls.
questions.more: It became independent under the 1929 Lateran Treaty.
questions.source: Britannica 'List of the smallest countries by area'; Remitly, 'What is the Smallest Country in the World?'

questions.cat: Europe · country that dissolved a royal union without war (e
questions.dimension: country that dissolved a royal union without war (early 1900s)
questions.q: Which country was joined to a larger Scandinavian neighbor until 1905, when a near-unanimous referendum peacefully dissolved the union?
questions.detail: People assume Scandinavian borders are ancient, but this country was joined to a larger neighbor until 1905, when a near-unanimous referendum dissolved the union peacefully.
questions.why: The union was with Sweden, not Denmark — and the split was settled by negotiation, with only 184 of 371,000-plus voters opposing dissolution.
questions.more: The country then invited a Danish prince to become its king.
questions.source: Wikipedia, 'Dissolution of the union between Norway and Sweden'; History Today

questions.cat: Europe · country whose modern independence dates to WWII (Na
questions.dimension: country whose modern independence dates to WWII (Nazi-occupied parent)
questions.q: Which country became a fully independent republic by referendum in 1944, breaking its royal union while its parent nation was under Nazi occupation?
questions.detail: It feels like one of Europe's oldest nations, but it only became a fully independent republic in 1944 — while its parent country was under Nazi occupation.
questions.why: The parent was Denmark, occupied by Germany at the time; the island broke its royal union and declared a republic by referendum, with over 95% in favor.
questions.more: Independence was proclaimed on June 17, still its national day.
questions.source: EBSCO Research Starters, 'Iceland Becomes a Republic'; nordics.info

questions.cat: Europe · century the Holy Roman Empire was dissolved
questions.dimension: century the Holy Roman Empire was dissolved
questions.q: The name sounds medieval, but in which century was the Holy Roman Empire actually abolished, under pressure from Napoleon?
questions.detail: The name sounds medieval, so people guess it ended centuries earlier — but this thousand-year empire was actually abolished in 1806, pressured by Napoleon.
questions.why: Reflex ties 'Holy Roman Empire' to the Middle Ages; it survived into the era of Napoleon, ending after the defeat at Austerlitz.
questions.more: Emperor Francis II dissolved it partly to deny Napoleon the title.
questions.source: Britannica, 'End of the Holy Roman Empire'; Wikipedia, 'Dissolution of the Holy Roman Empire'

questions.cat: Europe · century the Peace of Westphalia reshaped European s
questions.dimension: century the Peace of Westphalia reshaped European sovereignty
questions.q: In which century was the Peace of Westphalia signed, the treaties often called the birth of the modern nation-state system that ended a long religious war?
questions.detail: This landmark set of treaties — often called the birth of the modern nation-state system — was signed in 1648, ending a devastating thirty-year religious war.
questions.why: Many place 'modern statehood' later, in the Enlightenment or Napoleonic era; it actually dates to the mid-1600s.
questions.more: It ended the Thirty Years' War (1618-1648).
questions.source: World History Encyclopedia, 'Peace of Westphalia'; germanculture.com.ua

questions.cat: Europe · decade the Berlin Wall was built
questions.dimension: decade the Berlin Wall was built
questions.q: People link it to the start of the Cold War, but in which decade was the Berlin Wall actually built?
questions.detail: People link the Wall to the early Cold War's start, but it didn't go up until August 1961 — sixteen years after WWII ended.
questions.why: The Cold War divide began in the late 1940s, so reflex guesses the 1940s or 1950s; the Wall is a 1960s structure.
questions.more: It stood 28 years, falling in November 1989.
questions.source: Britannica, 'Berlin Wall'; National Geographic

questions.cat: Europe · decade a European democracy ended a long right-wing
questions.dimension: decade a European democracy ended a long right-wing dictatorship by military coup
questions.q: In which decade did Portugal's long Estado Novo dictatorship, Western Europe's longest, fall in the near-bloodless Carnation Revolution?
questions.detail: It sounds like ancient history, but Portugal's Estado Novo dictatorship — Western Europe's longest — was only toppled in 1974, in the near-bloodless Carnation Revolution.
questions.why: People assume Europe's dictatorships all fell in 1945; this one outlived WWII by almost three decades.
questions.more: Spain's transition followed Franco's death in 1975.
questions.source: Wikipedia, 'Carnation Revolution'; Real Instituto Elcano

questions.cat: Europe · NATO founding-era member among Mediterranean states
questions.dimension: NATO founding-era member among Mediterranean states (early joiner)
questions.q: Which Mediterranean country joined NATO in the alliance's very first expansion in 1952, decades before its neighbor Spain across the sea?
questions.detail: You might assume Mediterranean states joined NATO late, but this country joined in 1952, in the alliance's very first expansion — decades before its neighbor across the sea.
questions.why: Spain didn't join until 1982, so reflex lumps southern Europe together as latecomers; Greece (with Turkey) joined in 1952.
questions.more: Spain's much later 1982 entry came after Franco.
questions.source: history.state.gov, 'NATO 1949'; NATO, 'A short history of NATO'

questions.cat: Europe · decade a major Western European country joined NATO
questions.dimension: decade a major Western European country joined NATO (late joiner)
questions.q: NATO formed in 1949, but in which decade did Spain finally join, held back for years by its own dictatorship?
questions.detail: NATO formed in 1949, but one large Western European democracy didn't join until 1982 — held back for decades by its own dictatorship.
questions.why: People assume all of Western Europe joined at the founding; Spain was kept out under Franco and joined only in 1982.
questions.more: It later briefly debated leaving, but a 1986 referendum confirmed membership.
questions.source: Wikipedia, 'Enlargement of NATO'; NATO history

questions.cat: Europe · count of countries that adopted euro cash on day on
questions.dimension: count of countries that adopted euro cash on day one (2002)
questions.q: When euro notes and coins first reached people's pockets on 1 January 2002, how many countries made the switch?
questions.detail: The eurozone is large today, but when euro notes and coins first hit pockets on 1 January 2002, only twelve countries made the switch.
questions.why: People conflate today's ~20-member eurozone with the original cash launch; the 2002 changeover was twelve states.
questions.more: Greece had just qualified to join that first group.
questions.source: European Central Bank, 'Initial changeover (2002)'; OeNB

questions.cat: Europe · count of independent countries that emerged from fo
questions.dimension: count of independent countries that emerged from former Yugoslavia
questions.q: When the single federation of Yugoslavia broke apart in the 1990s and 2000s, how many separate independent countries did it eventually split into?
questions.detail: A single federation split apart in the 1990s and 2000s into this many separate countries — more than most people remember to count.
questions.why: People stop at four or five; the full set is Slovenia, Croatia, Bosnia, Serbia, Montenegro, North Macedonia, and Kosovo.
questions.more: Kosovo, the last, declared independence in 2008.
questions.source: history.state.gov, 'The Breakup of Yugoslavia'; The Institute of World Politics

questions.cat: Europe · country whose republic dates to the late 1940s (pos
questions.dimension: country whose republic dates to the late 1940s (post-Commonwealth split)
questions.q: Which country won home rule in 1922 but did not formally declare itself a republic and cut its last ties to the British crown until 1949?
questions.detail: It won home rule in 1922, but this country didn't formally declare itself a republic and cut its last ties to the British crown until 1949.
questions.why: Reflex dates full independence to the 1922 Free State; the republic — leaving the Commonwealth — came in 1949.
questions.more: It had already adopted a new constitution as 'Éire' in 1937.
questions.source: EBSCO, 'Irish Free State Is Proclaimed'; Secret Ireland

questions.cat: Europe · country last to join the EU's Cold War-divided east
questions.dimension: country last to join the EU's Cold War-divided eastern wave (with Romania)
questions.q: Most ex-communist states joined the EU in 2004, but two were held back to 2007 over reforms. Which Black Sea country joined alongside Romania in that later wave?
questions.detail: Most ex-communist states joined the EU in 2004, but two were held back to 2007 over reforms — this Black Sea country was one of them.
questions.why: Reflex lumps all the 'eastern enlargement' states into 2004; Bulgaria and Romania came three years later, in 2007.
questions.more: They had joined NATO earlier, in 2004.
questions.source: Wikipedia, '2007 enlargement of the European Union'; NBC News

questions.cat: Europe · century the Reformation began (split of Western Chr
questions.dimension: century the Reformation began (split of Western Christianity)
questions.q: In which century did the Reformation begin, the religious upheaval that split Western Christianity after a German monk reportedly posted 95 theses?
questions.detail: This religious upheaval that split Western Christianity began in 1517, when a German monk reportedly posted 95 theses against church practices.
questions.why: Some place it in the Middle Ages or the later 1600s; it's a 16th-century event tied to Martin Luther.
questions.more: It is conventionally dated to 1517.
questions.source: History.com, 'The Reformation'; Deutschlandmuseum

questions.cat: Europe · year a peaceful 'velvet' split created two countrie
questions.dimension: year a peaceful 'velvet' split created two countries
questions.q: In which decade did Czechoslovakia peacefully dissolve into two separate states in the 'Velvet Divorce'?
questions.detail: While Yugoslavia tore itself apart violently, one Central European federation dissolved peacefully into two states on 1 January 1993.
questions.why: People assume the Cold War's end split everything in 1989-1991; Czechoslovakia's 'Velvet Divorce' came a bit later, in 1993.
questions.more: It produced the Czech Republic and Slovakia.
questions.source: Britannica, 'Velvet Divorce'; ADST

questions.cat: Europe · year the Ottoman Empire was formally abolished
questions.dimension: year the Ottoman Empire was formally abolished
questions.q: Long called 'the sick man of Europe,' in which decade was the Ottoman sultanate actually abolished, just after World War I?
questions.detail: Its decline gets called 'the sick man of Europe' for a century, but the Ottoman sultanate wasn't actually abolished until 1922, just after WWI.
questions.why: People imagine it collapsed in the 1800s; the empire endured until the early 1920s, replaced by the Republic of Turkey in 1923.
questions.more: The last sultan left Constantinople by British warship.
questions.source: Wikipedia, 'Abolition of the Ottoman sultanate'; Britannica

questions.cat: Europe · year Austria-Hungary, the dual empire, dissolved
questions.dimension: year Austria-Hungary, the dual empire, dissolved
questions.q: In which decade did the Austro-Hungarian dual empire, which helped trigger World War I, actually dissolve?
questions.detail: This sprawling Central European empire that helped trigger WWI didn't survive it — the Austro-Hungarian union dissolved in 1918.
questions.why: Its end is easy to misplace into the 1920s peace-treaty era; the union itself broke apart in 1918, as the war ended.
questions.more: Treaties at Saint-Germain and Trianon formalized it by 1920.
questions.source: Wikipedia, 'Dissolution of Austria-Hungary'; Kafkadesk

questions.cat: Europe · decade the Warsaw Pact was founded
questions.dimension: decade the Warsaw Pact was founded
questions.q: NATO formed in 1949, but in which decade was the Soviet-led Warsaw Pact founded in response?
questions.detail: The Soviet-led military alliance is core to the Cold War, but it wasn't created until 1955 — six years after NATO.
questions.why: People assume the two blocs formed together in the late 1940s; the Warsaw Pact came in 1955, partly reacting to West Germany joining NATO.
questions.more: Founding members included East Germany and Bulgaria.
questions.source: History.com, 'Formation of NATO and Warsaw Pact'; nato.int

questions.cat: Europe · Largest island in the Baltic Sea
questions.dimension: Largest island in the Baltic Sea
questions.q: The Baltic Sea's largest island is Zealand, home to Copenhagen and more than twice the size of Sweden's famous Gotland. Which country does it belong to?
questions.detail: Quiz reflex says Gotland (Sweden), but the Baltic's largest island is Zealand — about 7,031 sq km versus Gotland's ~2,994 — and it belongs to this country.
questions.why: Gotland is famous as 'Sweden's largest island,' so people stop there; Zealand, home to Copenhagen, is more than twice its size and is the true Baltic record-holder.
questions.more: Zealand is also Europe's 13th-largest island by area.
questions.source: Wikipedia, Zealand; WorldAtlas, Largest Islands in the Baltic Sea

questions.cat: Europe · Larger share of Bialowieza Forest
questions.dimension: Larger share of Bialowieza Forest
questions.q: Europe's last great primeval lowland forest straddles a border, but the bigger slice and the main bison stronghold lie in which country, not its more-famous Polish neighbor?
questions.detail: Europe's last great primeval lowland forest straddles a border; the bigger slice — and the bison stronghold — lies in this country, not its more-famous Polish neighbor.
questions.why: The forest is usually tagged 'Poland' in travel coverage, but the majority of its ~150,000 hectares sits on the Belarusian side (Belovezhskaya Pushcha); only ~62,000 ha is in Poland.
questions.more: Inscribed by UNESCO; it shelters Europe's heaviest land animal, the European bison.
questions.source: Britannica, Belovezhskaya Forest; UNESCO WHC #33

questions.cat: Europe · Deepest canyon in Europe
questions.dimension: Deepest canyon in Europe
questions.q: Europe's deepest river canyon, the Tara, plunges about 1,300 m and is the second-deepest on Earth. Which small Balkan country holds most of it, in its Durmitor park?
questions.detail: Europe's deepest river canyon plunges about 1,300 m — the continent's answer to a mini Grand Canyon — and most of it lies inside this small Balkan country's Durmitor park.
questions.why: Players reach for the Alps or a big-name country; the Tara River Canyon is in a small nation many overlook, and it's the second-deepest canyon on Earth.
questions.more: The Tara's water is clean enough to drink straight from the river along much of its course.
questions.source: Wikipedia, Tara River Canyon; Montenegro.travel (Tara Canyon)

questions.cat: Europe · Country with the most islands on Earth
questions.dimension: Country with the most islands on Earth
questions.q: Which country has more islands than any other nation on Earth, over 267,000, thanks to its fractured glacial coastline, though fewer than 1% are inhabited?
questions.detail: More islands than any nation in the world — over 267,000 — belong to this country, though fewer than 1% are inhabited.
questions.why: The intuitive guesses are tropical archipelagos like Indonesia or the Philippines; the record belongs to a cold Nordic state thanks to its fractured glacial coastline.
questions.more: Neighboring Norway and Finland rank second and third.
questions.source: Statista (islands by country); World Population Review (Number of Islands by Country)

questions.cat: Europe · Largest natural lake in Central Europe
questions.dimension: Largest natural lake in Central Europe
questions.q: Central Europe's largest lake, nicknamed a national 'sea' and covering about 590 sq km though only around 3 m deep, lies in which landlocked plains country?
questions.detail: Central Europe's largest lake, nicknamed a national 'sea,' covers about 590 sq km in this country despite averaging only ~3 m deep.
questions.why: Mountain-rimmed alpine lakes come to mind, but the regional size record is a broad, shallow lowland lake — Balaton — in a landlocked plains country.
questions.more: Its shallowness makes it one of the warmest large lakes in Europe in summer.
questions.source: Britannica, Lake Balaton; Lakepedia (Balaton)

questions.cat: Europe · Largest island belonging to Greece
questions.dimension: Largest island belonging to Greece
questions.q: Of Greece's roughly 6,000 islands, which is the largest by far at about 8,340 sq km, more than twice the size of the runner-up Euboea?
questions.detail: Of Greece's roughly 6,000 islands, the biggest by far is this one at about 8,340 sq km — more than twice the size of the runner-up, Euboea.
questions.why: Famous holiday names like Rhodes, Corfu, or Santorini grab the guess, but none come close; the largest is the long mountainous southern island.
questions.more: It's also the most populous Greek island and the fifth-largest in the Mediterranean.
questions.source: Wikipedia, Crete; Touropia (largest islands in Greece)

questions.cat: Europe · highest share of Muslims in national population (Eu
questions.dimension: highest share of Muslims in national population (Europe)
questions.q: Reflex says Albania or Bosnia, but which European country actually has the highest share of Muslims in its population, around 93-96%?
questions.detail: About 93-96% of Kosovo's population is Muslim, the highest share of any country in Europe.
questions.why: The reflex is 'Albania' or 'Bosnia'; both are lower (Albania ~80%, Bosnia ~46%). Kosovo edges out Albania for the top spot.
questions.more: All of Europe's most-Muslim countries sit in the Balkans, a legacy of centuries of Ottoman rule.
questions.source: Pew Research Center; WorldAtlas (European Muslim populations)

questions.cat: Europe · only Romance language with official EU status from
questions.dimension: only Romance language with official EU status from a single Eastern-bloc member
questions.q: Surrounded by Slavic and Hungarian neighbors, which is the only Latin-descended (Romance) language that is the main official tongue of a former Eastern-bloc EU country?
questions.detail: Romanian is the only Romance (Latin-descended) language that is the main official language of a former Eastern-bloc EU state.
questions.why: Surrounded by Slavic and Hungarian neighbors, Romanian breaks the 'Eastern Europe = Slavic' reflex; it descends from the Latin of Roman Dacia.
questions.more: Its closest big sibling is Italian, yet it sits on the far side of a Slavic-speaking belt.
questions.source: Britannica; World Translation Center (Romanian language)

questions.cat: Europe · non-euro EU member whose currency is the koruna
questions.dimension: non-euro EU member whose currency is the koruna
questions.q: People reflex to Denmark or Sweden, but which EU member that has never adopted the euro still uses a currency called the koruna (and is not Slovakia)?
questions.detail: Czechia (the Czech Republic) keeps the Czech koruna; it has never adopted the euro.
questions.why: People reflex to Denmark or Sweden for 'EU but not the euro'; the koruna clue points specifically to Czechia, not Slovakia (which is on the euro).
questions.more: After Bulgaria joined the euro in 2026, only six EU states kept their own money.
questions.source: Britannica; European Union (countries using the euro)

questions.cat: Europe · number of EU countries using the euro (2026)
questions.dimension: number of EU countries using the euro (2026)
questions.q: As of 2026, after Bulgaria's January entry, how many of the EU's 27 member states use the euro?
questions.detail: As of 2026, 21 of the EU's 27 member states use the euro.
questions.why: Reflex is to guess the full 27 or a round number; Bulgaria's January 2026 entry made it exactly 21, with six members still outside.
questions.more: Denmark holds a formal opt-out; the rest are pledged to join once they qualify.
questions.source: Visual Capitalist (Eurozone 2026); Al Jazeera

questions.cat: Europe · Europe's only language isolate (no relatives anywhe
questions.dimension: Europe's only language isolate (no relatives anywhere)
questions.q: Finnish and Hungarian feel odd but belong to a family, while which language of the western Pyrenees is Europe's only language isolate, with no known relatives anywhere?
questions.detail: Basque (Euskara), spoken in the western Pyrenees, is the only language in Europe with no known relatives in any family.
questions.why: Finnish/Hungarian feel 'odd' but belong to the Uralic family; Basque belongs to no family at all, predating the spread of Indo-European.
questions.more: Roughly 900,000 people speak it across the Spanish and French Basque Country.
questions.source: Britannica (Basque language); Etxepare Basque Institute

questions.cat: Europe · world's largest exporter of cut flowers
questions.dimension: world's largest exporter of cut flowers
questions.q: Flowers seem like they could come from many warm climates, but which cool northern country, using greenhouses and auctions, exports nearly half the world's cut flowers?
questions.detail: The Netherlands accounts for nearly half of the world's cut-flower exports and most of its flower bulbs.
questions.why: Flowers feel like they could come from many warm climates; instead a cool northern country with greenhouses and auctions dominates the trade.
questions.more: Around 77% of the world's traded flower bulbs originate there.
questions.source: Skyminder (Netherlands flower trade); Holland Trade & Invest

questions.cat: Europe · highest coffee consumption per person in the world
questions.dimension: highest coffee consumption per person in the world
questions.q: Italy and the espresso cultures are the reflex, but which quiet Nordic country drinks the most coffee per person on Earth, around 10-12 kg a year?
questions.detail: Finns drink the most coffee per capita on Earth, around 10-12 kg per person a year.
questions.why: Italy and the espresso cultures are the reflex; instead a quiet Nordic country leads, where a workday coffee break is effectively a right.
questions.more: The other top coffee drinkers per capita are also Nordic.
questions.source: International Coffee Organization via Helgi Library; thisisFINLAND

questions.cat: Europe · the living European language closest to Old Norse
questions.dimension: the living European language closest to Old Norse
questions.q: The reflex is Norwegian, but which living European language has changed so little that speakers can still read 800-year-old medieval sagas with relative ease?
questions.detail: Icelandic has changed so little that speakers can still read 800-year-old medieval sagas with relative ease.
questions.why: The reflex is Norwegian (Norse homeland), but isolated Icelandic preserved Old Norse grammar far better; its closest sibling is Faroese.
questions.more: Iceland actively coins native words rather than borrowing, slowing change.
questions.source: Britannica (Icelandic language); EBSCO Research Starters
